title: Gradient Manipulation in Distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent with Strategic Agents: Truthful Incentives with Convergence Guarantees
abstract: Distributed learning has gained significant attention due to its advantages in scalability, privacy, and fault tolerance.In this paradigm, multiple agents collaboratively train a global model by exchanging parameters only with their neighbors. However, a key vulnerability of existing distributed learning approaches is their implicit assumption that all agents behave honestly during gradient updates. In real-world scenarios, this assumption often breaks down, as selfish or strategic agents may be incentivized to manipulate gradients for personal gain, ultimately compromising the final learning outcome. In this work, we propose a fully distributed payment mechanism that, for the first time, guarantees both truthful behaviors and accurate convergence in distributed stochastic gradient descent. This represents a significant advancement, as it overcomes two major limitations of existing truthfulness mechanisms for collaborative learning:(1) reliance on a centralized server for payment collection, and (2) sacrificing convergence accuracy to guarantee truthfulness. In addition to characterizing the convergence rate under general convex and strongly convex conditions, we also prove that our approach guarantees the cumulative gain that an agent can obtain through strategic behavior remains finite, even as the number of iterations approaches infinity--a property unattainable by most existing truthfulness mechanisms. Our experimental results on standard machine learning tasks, evaluated on benchmark datasets, confirm the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

sections.heading: IIntroduction
sections.text:
Recent years have witnessed significant advances in distributed methods for collaborative optimization and learning [ 35 , 31 , 50 , 34 ] . By distributing both data and computational resources across multiple agents, distributed methods leverage the combined computing power of multiple devices to collaboratively train a global model without the need for a centralized server. Compared with server-assisted collaborative learning 1 1 1 We use “server-assisted collaborative learning” to refer to collaborative learning involving a centralized server or aggregator, with federated learning as a representative example. , distributed learning avoids monopolistic control and single points of failure [ 48 ] , and hence, is widely applied in areas such as distributed machine learning [ 44 ] , multi-robot coordination [ 41 ] , and wireless networks [ 9 ] .

However, almost all existing distributed learning approaches implicitly assume that all participating agents act truthfully (see, e.g., [ 49 , 37 , 39 , 30 , 1 , 52 , 32 , 58 , 56 , 42 , 26 ] ), which is essential for their successful execution. This premise becomes untenable in practical scenarios where participating agents are strategic and self-interested. In such cases, participating agents may manipulate gradient updates to maximize their own utilities, ultimately undermining the performance of collaborative learning. For example, in distributed learning with heterogeneous data distributions, an agent may inflate its local gradient updates to skew the final model in favor of its own data distribution [ 7 , 8 ] . Similarly, in a shared market, a firm may inject noise into its shared data to degrade the quality of other firms’ predictive model training, thereby maintaining competitive advantage [ 19 ] . More motivating examples are provided in Section II-B . Such untruthful behaviors pose a significant threat to the performance of existing distributed learning and optimization algorithms (as demonstrated by our experimental results in Fig. 2 ).

To mitigate strategic behaviors of participating agents in collaborative learning, several approaches have been proposed, which can be broadly categorized into incentive-based approaches [ 28 , 5 , 51 , 2 , 17 , 40 , 11 , 47 , 21 , 54 , 25 , 3 , 12 , 20 , 18 , 15 ] and joint-differential-privacy (JDP)-based approaches [ 24 , 23 , 57 , 55 ] . However, all existing approaches rely on a centralized server to collect information from all agents and then execute a truthfulness mechanism [ 3 ] . For example, the Vickrey–Clarke–Groves (VCG) mechanism [ 45 , 16 , 22 ] , a well-known incentive-based approach, requires a centralized server to aggregate true gradients/functions from all agents to compute the corresponding monetary transfers. Similarly, JDP-based approaches require a centralized server to collect iteration variables from all agents in order to compute the necessary noise [ 24 , 23 , 57 , 55 ] 2 2 2 Although the recent work by [ 13 ] proposes a JDP-based truthfulness approach for distributed aggregative optimization, it is limited to scenarios where each agent’s objective function depends on an aggregative term of others’ optimization variables. Furthermore, due to differential-privacy noise injection, the approach achieves truthfulness at the cost of compromised optimization accuracy (see Theorem 3 in [ 13 ] for details). . To the best of our knowledge, no existing approaches can effectively incentivize truthful behaviors in fully distributed optimization and learning.

sections.heading: I-ARelated literature
sections.text:
Incentive-based truthfulness approaches. Truthfulness in statistical (mean) estimation has been addressed using incentive/payment mechanisms [ 5 , 17 , 11 ] . These mechanisms are typically one-shot, where agents choose their strategies once and a centralized server broadcasts payments accordingly, which renders them inapplicable to multi-round, gradient-based distributed learning algorithms. Based on the well-known VCG mechanism [ 45 , 16 , 22 ] , truthfulness results have also been reported for federated learning (see, e.g., [ 7 , 19 , 8 , 54 , 25 , 3 , 12 ] ). However, the VCG mechanism relies on a server to calculate and collect monetary payments, which makes it inapplicable in a fully distributed setting. Moreover, VCG-based approaches are not budget-balanced and often involve surplus payments [ 47 , 21 , 54 , 25 , 3 ] , which further limits their practicality. It is worth noting that many results have discussed incentive mechanisms for encouraging agents’ contributions of data/resources in collaborative learning [ 5 , 54 , 25 ] . However, those results do not consider agents’ strategic manipulation on iterative updates for personal gains.

JDP-based truthfulness approaches. JDP-based approaches incentivize truthful behavior by injecting noise into algorithmic outputs, thereby masking the impact of any single agent’s misreporting on the final model and promoting truthfulness [ 24 , 23 , 57 , 55 ] . However, these approaches require a centralized server to collect local optimization variables from all agents to determine the needed noise amplitude, which makes it infeasible in a fully distributed setting. Moreover, JDP-based approaches have to compromise convergence accuracy to ensure truthfulness [ 13 ] , which is undesirable in accuracy-sensitive applications.

sections.heading: I-BContributions
sections.text:
In this article, we propose a distributed payment mechanism that
guarantees both truthful behavior of agents and accurate convergence in distributed stochastic gradient methods. The main contributions are summarized as follows (Table I highlights the contributions and their comparison with existing truthfulness results):

• We propose a fully distributed payment mechanism that incentivizes truthful behaviors among interacting strategic agents in distributed stochastic gradient methods. This represents a substantial breakthrough, as existing truthfulness mechanisms (in, e.g., [ 7 , 19 , 8 , 28 , 5 , 51 , 2 , 17 , 40 , 11 , 47 , 21 , 54 , 25 , 3 , 12 , 20 , 18 , 15 , 24 , 23 , 57 , 55 ] ) all rely on a centralized server to aggregate local information from agents. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first payment mechanism for distributed gradient descent without the assistance of any centralized server. • Our payment mechanism guarantees that the incentive for a strategic agent to deviate from truthful behaviors diminishes to zero over time (see Lemma 1 ). Building on this, we further prove that the cumulative gain that an agent can obtain from its strategic behaviors remains finite, even when the number of iterations tends to infinity (see Theorem 2 ). This stands in sharp contrast to existing incentive-based approaches for federated learning in [ 7 , 19 , 8 ] , which cannot eliminate agents’ incentives to behave untruthfully–resulting in a cumulative gain that grows unbounded when the number of iterations tends to infinity. • In addition to ensuring diminishing incentives for untruthful behavior in distributed gradient descent, our payment mechanism also guarantees accurate convergence, even in the presence of persistent gradient manipulation by agents (see Theorem 1 ). This is in stark contrast to existing JDP-based truthfulness results in [ 24 , 23 , 57 , 55 , 13 ] and incentive-based truthfulness results in [ 19 , 7 , 8 ] , all of which are subject to an optimization error. We analyze the convergence rates of distributed gradient descent under our payment mechanism for general convex and strongly convex objective functions. This is more comprehensive than existing truthfulness results in [ 19 , 7 , 8 , 3 ] that focus solely on the strongly convex case. • Different from most existing VCG-based approaches (in, e.g., [ 47 , 21 , 54 , 25 , 3 ] ), which cannot ensure budget-balance (the total payments from all agents sum to zero, a property essential for the financial sustainability and practical scalability of the mechanism), our payment mechanism is budget-balanced. This is significant in a fully distributed setting since no centralized server is used to manage subsidies or surplus. • We evaluate the performance of our truthful mechanism using representative distributed learning tasks, including image classification on the FeMNIST dataset and next-character prediction on the Shakespeare dataset. The experimental results confirm the effectiveness of our approach.

The organization of the paper is as follows. Sec. II introduces the problem formulation, presents motivating examples, and formalizes the used game-theoretic framework. Sec. III proposes the distributed payment mechanism. Sec. IV analyzes convergence rate and establishes incentive compatibility guarantees. Sec. V presents experimental results. Finally, Sec. VI concludes the paper.

sections.heading: II-ADistributed optimization and learning
sections.text:
We consider N ≥ 2 N\geq 2 agents participating in distributed optimization and learning, each possessing a private dataset whose distribution can be heterogeneous across the agents. The goal is for all agents to cooperatively find a solution θ ∗ \theta^{*} to the following stochastic optimization problem: min θ ∈ ℝ n ⁡ F ​ ( θ ) = 1 N ​ ∑ i = 1 N f i ​ ( θ ) , f i ​ ( θ ) = 𝔼 ζ i ∼ 𝒫 i ​ [ l ​ ( θ ; ζ i ) ] , \min_{\theta\in\mathbb{R}^{n}}~F(\theta)=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}f_{i}(\theta),\quad f_{i}(\theta)=\mathbb{E}_{\zeta_{i}\sim\mathcal{P}_{i}}[l(\theta;\zeta_{i})], (1) where θ ∈ ℝ n \theta\in\mathbb{R}^{n} denotes a global model parameter and ζ i \zeta_{i} is a random data sample of agent i i drawn from its local data distribution 𝒫 i \mathcal{P}_{i} . The loss function l ​ ( θ ; ζ i ) : ℝ n × ℝ n ↦ ℝ l(\theta;\zeta_{i}):\mathbb{R}^{n}\!\times\!\mathbb{R}^{n}\!\mapsto\!\mathbb{R} is assumed to be differentiable in θ \theta for every ζ i \zeta_{i} and the local objective function f i ​ ( θ ) f_{i}(\theta) of agent i i can be nonconvex.

In real-world applications, the data distribution 𝒫 i \mathcal{P}_{i} is typically unknown. Hence, each agent i i can only access a noisy estimate of the gradient ∇ f i ​ ( θ i , t ) \nabla f_{i}(\theta_{i,t}) , computed at its current local model parameter θ i , t \theta_{i,t} using the available local data. For example,
at each iteration t t , agent i i samples a batch of B ≥ 1 B\geq 1 data points and computes a gradient estimate as g i ​ ( θ i , t ) = 1 B ​ ∑ j = 1 B ∇ l ​ ( θ i , t ; ζ i ​ j ) g_{i}(\theta_{i,t})=\frac{1}{B}\sum_{j=1}^{B}\nabla l(\theta_{i,t};\zeta_{ij}) . Using this gradient estimate g i ​ ( θ i , t ) g_{i}(\theta_{i,t}) , along with the model parameters { θ j , t } j ∈ 𝒩 i \{\theta_{j,t}\}_{j\in\mathcal{N}_{i}} received from its neighbors, agent i i updates its local parameter according to a distributed optimization/learning algorithm.

Existing distributed optimization/learning algorithms (see, e.g., [ 49 , 37 , 39 , 30 , 1 , 52 , 32 , 58 , 56 , 42 , 26 ] ) universally assume that participating agents are honest and behave truthfully. However, this assumption may be unrealistic in real-world scenarios, where agents can behave selfishly or strategically. For example, a strategic agent may amplify its gradient estimates to bias the final model parameter in favor of its own data distribution, or inject noise into its gradient information to degrade the performance of other agents’ models for competitive advantage. (We provide additional motivating examples to illustrate how agents can benefit from gradient manipulation in distributed least squares and distributed mean estimation in Section II-B .) Such strategic gradient manipulation can significantly degrade the learning performance of existing distributed learning algorithms (as evidenced by our experimental results in Fig. 2 ).

Next, we discuss the classical Distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (Distributed SGD) in the presence of gradient manipulation by a strategic agent i ∈ [ N ] i\in[N] .

Distributed SGD in the presence of strategic behavior. At each iteration t t , each agent i i strategically chooses a manipulated gradient m i , t m_{i,t} , which, in general, is a function of agent i i ’s true gradient g i ​ ( θ i , t ) g_{i}(\theta_{i,t}) . Using the manipulated gradient m i , t m_{i,t} and the model parameters { θ j , t } j ∈ 𝒩 i \{\theta_{j,t}\}_{j\in\mathcal{N}_{i}} received from its neighbors, each agent i i updates its local model parameter according to Algorithm 1 .

It is worth noting that we focus on gradient manipulation rather than model-parameter manipulation for two reasons. First, any manipulation of model parameters shared among agents effectively corresponds to some form of alteration in the gradient estimates, as proven in Corollary 1 in Appendix E. Second, gradient manipulation is
the most direct and practically effective strategy for a strategic agent to increase its personal gain. Specifically, by upscaling its own gradient estimates, an agent can increase the influence of its local data on the cooperative learning process, thereby
pulling the final model parameter closer to the minimizer of its local objective function and reducing its own cost. On the other hand, by injecting noise into its gradient estimates, an agent can reduce the usefulness of its data to its neighbors, degrading their model performance and gaining competitive advantage. In comparison, manipulating model parameters does not provide a clear strategic benefit. For these reasons, we focus on gradient manipulation as the primary form of untruthful or strategic behavior of participating agents.

sections.heading: II-BMotivating examples
sections.text:
In this subsection, we first use a distributed least-squares problem to show that strategic agents can lower their individual costs by amplifying local gradients, at the expense of network performance. We then consider a distributed mean estimation problem with stochastic gradients to show that agents can further improve their payoffs by manipulating gradient updates in Algorithm 1 , again sacrificing network performance.

Example 1 and Example 2 demonstrate that a self-interested agent can benefit from its strategic gradient manipulation while degrading the network-level performance.

In addition to decentralized least squares problems, similar truthfulness issues also emerge in collaborative machine learning [ 19 ] , distributed electric-vehicle charging [ 13 ] , and many
other collaborative optimization applications [ 15 ] .

To quantitatively analyze strategic interactions among agents, we adopt a game-theoretic framework that explicitly defines each agent’s strategic behavior, reward, payment, and resulting net utility.

sections.heading: II-CGame-theoretic framework
sections.text:
Strategic behaviors and action space. A self-interested agent can enhance its individual outcome through two strategic behaviors: amplifying its gradient estimates to bias the final model parameter in favor of its own data distribution, and injecting noise to degrade the performance of other agents’ model parameters for competitive advantage. Both strategic behaviors can be modeled as an agent’s action at each iteration. Formally, we
define the action space for each strategic agent i ∈ [ N ] i\in[N] in distributed optimization/learning as follows: 𝒜 i = { α i | α i ​ ( g i ​ ( θ ) ) = a i ​ g i ​ ( θ ) + b i ​ ξ i , a i ≥ 1 , b i ∈ ℝ } , \!\mathcal{A}_{i}\!=\!\{\alpha_{i}|\alpha_{i}(g_{i}(\theta))\!=\!a_{i}g_{i}(\theta)\!+\!b_{i}\xi_{i},~a_{i}\!\geq 1,~b_{i}\!\in\mathbb{R}\}, (20) where g i ​ ( θ ) g_{i}(\theta) represents agent i i ’s true gradient estimate and ξ i \xi_{i} is a zero-mean noise vector with bounded variance. The scaling factor a i a_{i} quantifies the degree of gradient amplification, while the noise factor b i b_{i} specifies the magnitude of noise injection, both of which can be strategically chosen by agent i i at each iteration. For any action space 𝒜 i \mathcal{A}_{i} , we assume that it includes the identity mapping, which maps g i g_{i} to itself with probability one. Hence, truthfulness is always a feasible action.

The action space defined in ( 20 ) is designed to capture the strategic behavior of agents in distributed learning rather than arbitrary malicious attacks. To this end, we consider a i ≥ 1 a_{i}\geq 1 and ignore a i < 1 a_{i}<1 , because the latter typically reduces the influence of agent i i ’s local data on cooperative learning, making it a non-utility-improving choice for a rational agent. This focus on strategic, utility-driven behavior excludes general malicious attacks, which are typically disruptive and do not align with an agent’s goal of improving its outcome within the learning framework. In addition, compared with the action spaces defined in existing results on federated learning, such as [ 19 ] that only considers noise injection (i.e., fixing a i = 1 a_{i}=1 ) and [ 7 ] that only considers gradient amplification (i.e., fixing b i = 0 b_{i}=0 ), our formulation accounts for a broader range of strategic manipulations.

Rewards. We denote a distributed learning algorithm as ℳ \mathcal{M} . At each iteration t t , agent i i chooses an action α i , t ∈ 𝒜 i \alpha_{i,t}\in\mathcal{A}_{i} . This action produces a (manipulated) gradient m i , t = α i , t ​ ( g i ​ ( θ i , t ) ) m_{i,t}=\alpha_{i,t}(g_{i}(\theta_{i,t})) , which is then used by agent i i in the update step of ℳ \mathcal{M} . Considering T + 1 T+1 iterations of ℳ \mathcal{M} , we let 𝜶 i = { α i , t } t = 0 T \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{i}=\{\alpha_{i,t}\}_{t=0}^{T} denote the action trajectory of agent i i from iteration 0 to iteration T T , 𝜽 j = { θ j , t } t = 0 T \boldsymbol{\theta}_{j}=\{\theta_{j,t}\}_{t=0}^{T} denote the model-parameter trajectory of agent j j , and 𝜽 − i = { 𝜽 j } j ∈ 𝒩 i \boldsymbol{\theta}_{-i}=\{\boldsymbol{\theta}_{j}\}_{j\in\mathcal{N}_{i}} denote the collection of model-parameter trajectories received by agent i i from all its neighbors. Given an initial model parameter θ i , 0 \theta_{i,0} , an action trajectory 𝜶 i \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{i} , and a collection of model-parameter trajectories 𝜽 − i \boldsymbol{\theta}_{-i} , an implementation of ℳ \mathcal{M} generates a final model parameter θ i , T + 1 = ℳ ​ ( θ i , 0 , 𝜶 i , 𝜽 − i ) \theta_{i,T+1}=\mathcal{M}(\theta_{i,0},\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{i},\boldsymbol{\theta}_{-i}) for agent i i . We denote the reward that agent i i obtains from its final objective (cost) function value as R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ) ) R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1})) .

In a minimization problem (see ( 1 )), the reward function R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ ) ) R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta)) increases as the objective function f i ​ ( θ ) f_{i}(\theta) decreases. Therefore, a self-interested agent can boost its reward by biasing the final solution toward the minimizer of its local objective function. Common choices for R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ ) ) R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta)) include the linear function R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ ) ) = − f i ​ ( θ ) R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta))\!=\!-f_{i}(\theta) and the sigmoid-like function R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ ) ) = ( 1 + e − 1 / f i ​ ( θ ) ) − 1 R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta))\!=\!(1+e^{-1/f_{i}(\theta)})^{-1} [ 8 ] . We allow different agents to have different reward functions.

Payments and net utilities. To mitigate gradient manipulation by participating agents, we augment the distributed learning protocol with a payment mechanism. This mechanism can be computed efficiently and implemented in a fully decentralized manner between any pair of interacting agents (see details in Section III ). We denote the augmented distributed learning protocol (with a payment mechanism) by ℳ p \mathcal{M}_{p} . The net utility of agent i i from executing ℳ p \mathcal{M}_{p} over T + 1 T+1 iterations is defined as follows: U i , 0 → T ℳ p ​ ( 𝜶 i , 𝜶 − i ) = R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ) ) − ∑ t = 0 T P i , t , \vskip-1.99997ptU_{i,0\text{\tiny$\to$}T}^{\mathcal{M}_{p}}(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{i},\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{-i})=R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}))-\sum_{t=0}^{T}\!P_{i,t},\vskip-1.99997pt (21) where P i , t P_{i,t} is the total net payment made by agent i i to all its neighbors, and 𝜶 − i = { 𝜶 j } j ≠ i \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{-i}=\{\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{j}\}_{j\neq i} denotes the action trajectories of all agents except agent i i .

We note that all existing incentive-based approaches for collaborative learning ensure truthfulness by incorporating a payment or penalty term into each agent’s net utility. Without such payments, a self-interested agent can freely manipulate its gradients to reduce its own loss and increase its rewards, thereby distorting the collaborative learning process. Therefore, ( 21 ) includes the cumulative payments of each agent in its net utility. Accordingly, a rational agent must consider both its rewards and payments when maximizing its net utility.

Next, we introduce two truthfulness-related concepts in our game-theoretic framework.

Definition 1 quantifies the truthfulness of an agent’s action in collaborative optimization/learning. It can be seen that a smaller δ \delta corresponds to a higher level of truthfulness in the agent’s action.

Definition 2 (also called ε \varepsilon -Bayesian-incentive compatibility) is a standard
and commonly used notion in the existing incentive-compatibility literature [ 33 , 4 , 7 , 8 ] . It implies that if a distributed optimization protocol is ε \varepsilon -incentive compatible, then the expected net utility that an agent can gain from any (possibly untruthful) action trajectory is at most ε \varepsilon greater than that obtained by being truthful in all iterations. Clearly, a smaller ε \varepsilon corresponds to a lower gain that an untruthful agent can obtain. In addition, according to the definition of ε \varepsilon -Nash equilibrium in [ 27 ] , if a distributed learning protocol is ε \varepsilon -incentive compatible, then the truthful action trajectory profile of all agents 𝒉 = ( 𝒉 i , 𝒉 − i ) \boldsymbol{h}=(\boldsymbol{h}_{i},\boldsymbol{h}_{-i}) forms an ε \varepsilon -Nash equilibrium (see Lemma 6 in Appendix E).

sections.heading: IIIPayment Mechanism Design for Distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent
sections.text:
In this section, we propose a fully distributed payment mechanism (see Mechanism 1 ) to incentivize truthful behaviors of participating agents in distributed stochastic gradient descent.

Mechanism 1 is implementable in a fully distributed manner without the assistance of any server or aggregator. At each iteration t t , if agent i i manipulates its gradient estimates such that its model-parameter increment ‖ θ i , t + 1 − 2 ​ θ i , t + θ i , t − 1 ‖ \|\theta_{i,t+1}-2\theta_{i,t}+\theta_{i,t-1}\| exceeds that of its neighbor agent j j , agent i i pays an amount P i , t j > 0 P_{i,t}^{j}>0 to agent j j (in this case, we denote the payment of agent j j as P j , t i = − P i , t j ) P_{j,t}^{i}=-P_{i,t}^{j}) . Conversely, if agent i i ’s model-parameter increment ‖ θ i , t + 1 − 2 ​ θ i , t + θ i , t − 1 ‖ \|\theta_{i,t+1}-2\theta_{i,t}+\theta_{i,t-1}\| is no greater than that of its neighbor agent j j , it receives a payment of amount P j , t i ≥ 0 P_{j,t}^{i}\geq 0 from agent j j (in this case, we denote the payment of agent i i as P i , t j = − P j , t i P_{i,t}^{j}=-P_{j,t}^{i} ). We emphasize that our payment mechanism can be readily applied to any first-order distributed gradient methods. For an agent i i , its total payment to all its neighbors at iteration t t is given by P i , t = ∑ j ∈ 𝒩 i P i , t j P_{i,t}=\sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}_{i}}P_{i,t}^{j} .

In Mechanism 1 , with both agents i i and j j having access to θ ι , t − 1 \theta_{\iota,t-1} , θ ι , t \theta_{\iota,t} , and θ ι , t + 1 \theta_{\iota,t+1} for ι ∈ { i , j } \iota\in\{i,j\} from the update of Algorithm 1 (note that θ ι , t + 1 \theta_{\iota,t+1} has been shared at the end of iteration t t ), the two agents can cross-verify the computed payment value, making the mechanism robust to unilateral manipulation. This represents a significant advance compared with the payment mechanism in [ 3 ] for server-assisted collaborative optimization, which requires all agents to truthfully report their local objective-function values for payment calculation—thereby creating a risk that strategic agents may manipulate the algorithmic update and the payment mechanism separately.

Our payment mechanism can effectively discourage agents from free-riding 3 3 3 Free-riding refers to the behavior in which agents skip computing gradients on their local data and instead update their parameters solely based on information received from neighbors. In this way, they benefit from the distributed optimization process without contributing their own data or gradients. . Specifically, the model-parameter increment ‖ θ i , t + 1 − 2 ​ θ i , t + θ i , t − 1 ‖ \|\theta_{i,t+1}-2\theta_{i,t}+\theta_{i,t-1}\| of agent i i depends on both the consensus errors and the (sign-indefinite) local gradients. Consequently, even if agent i i uses a zero (or low) gradient, there is no guarantee that ‖ θ i , t + 1 − 2 ​ θ i , t + θ i , t − 1 ‖ \|\theta_{i,t+1}-2\theta_{i,t}+\theta_{i,t-1}\| will be smaller than that of its neighbor j j , meaning that leveraging zero gradients does not reliably increase agent i i ’s payment gains. In fact, free-riding behavior (or using low gradients) invariably degrades agent i i ’s own reward R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T ) ) R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T})) , as it weakens the influence of agent i i ’s data in collaborative learning and ultimately leads to a worse final model for the agent itself. Hence, free-riding is not a utility-improving choice for a rational agent.

Our payment mechanism is conceptually inspired by the classical VCG mechanism [ 45 , 16 , 22 ] but has several fundamental differences: 1) conventional VCG mechanisms require a central server to calculate and collect payments [ 45 , 16 , 22 ] , whereas our mechanism operates in a pairwise fashion without reliance on any third party. As a result, it can be implemented in a fully distributed manner; 2) conventional VCG mechanisms are typically designed for one-shot games [ 28 , 51 , 2 , 40 ] . In contrast, our payment mechanism is naturally compatible with iterative algorithms, encompassing a wide range of distributed optimization/learning methods. In fact, an iterative algorithm setting inherently forms a multi-stage game, where agents repeatedly adjust actions, posing challenges for both truthfulness and convergence analysis; and 3) conventional VCG mechanisms are not budget-balanced (see, e.g., [ 47 , 21 , 54 , 25 , 3 ] ), whereas our payment mechanism is budget-balanced, i.e., ∑ i = 1 N P i , t = 0 \sum_{i=1}^{N}P_{i,t}=0 , making it financially sustainable and scalable in practice.

The existing approaches most closely related to ours are the payment mechanisms proposed by [ 7 , 8 ] . However, there are several fundamental differences: 1) our payment mechanism is implementable in a fully distributed manner, and hence, is applicable to arbitrary connected communication graphs, whereas the mechanisms in [ 7 , 8 ] rely on a centralized server to aggregate gradients from all agents, and thus operate only under a centralized communication structure; and 2) our payment mechanism achieves ε \varepsilon -incentive compatibility with a finite ε \varepsilon even in an infinite time horizon (see Theorem 2 ), whereas the incentive ε \varepsilon in [ 7 , 8 ] becomes unbounded as the number of iterations tends to infinity (see Claim 23 in [ 7 ] or Theorem 5.1 in [ 8 ] ), leading to a vanishing incentive compatibility guarantee over iterations.

sections.heading: IVConvergence Rate and Incentive-Compatibility Analysis
sections.text:
Assumption 1 is standard and commonly used in the distributed stochastic optimization/learning literature [ 10 , 29 , 43 ] . It is worth noting that we allow f i ​ ( θ ) f_{i}(\theta) to be convex, which is more general than the strongly convex assumption used in existing truthfulness results in [ 24 , 19 , 7 , 8 , 3 ] . Assumption 2 ensures that the communication graph is connected [ 38 ] .

Furthermore, for the sake of notational simplicity, we denote L R = max i ∈ [ N ] ⁡ { L R , i } L_{R}=\max_{i\in[N]}\{L_{R,i}\} , L f = max i ∈ [ N ] ⁡ { L f , i } L_{f}=\max_{i\in[N]}\{L_{f,i}\} , H = max i ∈ [ N ] ⁡ { H i } H=\max_{i\in[N]}\{H_{i}\} , and σ = max i ∈ [ N ] ⁡ { σ i } \sigma=\max_{i\in[N]}\{\sigma_{i}\} .

sections.heading: IV-AConvergence rate analysis
sections.text:
We first prove that, under Mechanism 1 , the incentive for a strategic agent in Algorithm 1 to deviate from truthful behavior diminishes to zero. We then analyze the convergence rates of Algorithm 1 in the presence of strategic behaviors, for strongly convex and general convex objective functions, respectively.

Lemma 1 proves that when the neighbors of agent i i are truthful, the incentive for agent i i to deviate from truthful behavior converges to zero. This stands in sharp contrast to existing payment mechanisms in [ 19 , 7 , 8 ] for federated learning, which guarantees only a bounded—yet non-diminishing—incentive for untruthful behavior at each iteration, thus leaving agents with a persistent motive to act untruthfully. Furthermore, Lemma 1 implies that by choosing an arbitrarily small δ > 0 \delta>0 , we can ensure that the optimal action of each agent i i is arbitrarily close to being fully truthful at every iteration. It is worth noting that since the differences in model-parameter increments in Mechanism 1 diminish to zero, we can ensure lim t → ∞ 𝔼 ​ [ P i , t ] = 0 \lim_{t\rightarrow\infty}\mathbb{E}[P_{i,t}]=0 (as shown in Corollary 2 in Appendix E). This guarantees that no payment is required from agent i i when it behaves truthfully as the number of iterations tends to infinity.

Theorem 1 proves that, even in the presence of strategic behaviors, our proposed Mechanism 1 ensures convergence to an exact
optimal solution θ ∗ \theta^{*} to the problem in ( 1 ) at rates 𝒪 ​ ( T − v ) \mathcal{O}(T^{-v}) and 𝒪 ​ ( T − ( 1 − v ) ) \mathcal{O}(T^{-(1-v)}) for strongly convex and general convex f i ​ ( θ ) f_{i}(\theta) , respectively. It is broader than existing server-assisted truthfulness results in [ 24 , 19 , 7 , 8 , 3 ] that focus solely on the strongly convex case. Moreover, our results are in stark contrast to existing JDP-based truthfulness results in [ 24 , 23 , 57 , 55 , 13 ] and incentive-based truthfulness results in [ 19 , 7 , 8 ] , all of which are subject to optimization errors.

sections.heading: IV-BIncentive-compatibility analysis
sections.text:
In addition to achieving accurate convergence, our fully distributed payment mechanism also simultaneously ensures that Algorithm 1 is ε \varepsilon -incentive compatible.

Theorem 2 ensures that the cumulative gain from
an agent i i ’s untruthful behaviors in Algorithm 1 remains finite, even when T → ∞ T\rightarrow\infty . This contrasts with existing truthfulness results for server-assisted
federated learning (e.g., [ 7 , 8 ] ), where ε \varepsilon explodes as the iteration proceeds, implying that truthfulness/incentive compatibility will eventually be lost.

Existing incentive-based truthfulness results for server-assisted federated learning in, e.g., [ 19 , 7 , 8 ] , do not provide simultaneous guarantees for both ε \varepsilon -incentive compatibility and accurate convergence. Specifically, the convergence analysis in [ 19 ] requires two conditions: (i) P ( ∃ t ≤ T : Π W ( θ t s − γ t m ¯ t ) ≠ θ t s − γ t m ¯ t ) ∈ 𝒪 ( 1 N ​ T ) P(\exists t\leq T:\Pi_{W}(\theta_{t}^{s}-\gamma_{t}\bar{m}_{t})\neq\theta_{t}^{s}-\gamma_{t}\bar{m}_{t})\in\mathcal{O}(\frac{1}{NT}) and (ii) the boundedness of W W (see Theorem 6.1 in [ 19 ] ), where θ t s \theta_{t}^{s} is the model parameter computed by the centralized server, W W is a projection set, γ t \gamma_{t} is the stepsize, and m ¯ t \bar{m}_{t} is the average (manipulated) gradients reported by all agents. Moreover, in the Appendix section “Discussion on the projection assumptions”, they state that Condition (i) can be guaranteed when W W grows at a rate of Ω ​ ( T ) \Omega(T) for general strongly convex functions, which is at odds with the boundedness requirement on W W in Condition (ii) when T T tends to infinity (their convergence error 𝒪 ​ ( 1 + M + ε 2 N ​ T ) + 𝒪 ​ ( 1 T 2 ) \mathcal{O}(\frac{1+M+\varepsilon^{2}}{NT})+\mathcal{O}(\frac{1}{T^{2}}) in Theorem 6.1 is strictly larger than 0 unless T T is allowed to approach infinity). Therefore, they did not provide a method for ensuring that both conditions hold simultaneously under general strongly convex objectives. A similar issue also exists in [ 7 ] (see Theorem 9 and footnote 2 therein). Although the convergence analysis in [ 8 ] removes these two conditions, its definition G = ∑ t = 1 T γ t ​ 𝒞 t G=\sum_{t=1}^{T}\gamma_{t}\sqrt{\mathcal{C}_{t}} in Theorem 5.1 implies that ε = 𝒪 ​ ( G ) \varepsilon=\mathcal{O}(G) is finite only when T T is finite, indicating that both its ε \varepsilon -incentive compatibility and convergence statements fail to hold in an infinite time horizon.

sections.heading: VExperiment Evaluation
sections.text:
We evaluate the effectiveness of our truthful mechanism using three representative distributed machine learning tasks: image classification on the FeMNIST dataset and next-character prediction on the Shakespeare dataset. The used datasets are from LEAF [ 6 ] .  For all experiments, we used FedLab [ 53 ] to migrate the dataset pipeline from LEAF’s TensorFlow-based workflow to a PyTorch-based workflow. In addition, each agent’s local dataset was split into 90 % 90\% for training and 10 % 10\% for testing. We considered five agents connected in a circle, where each agent communicates only with its two immediate neighbors. For the coupling matrix W W , we set w i ​ j = 0.3 w_{ij}=0.3 if agents i i and j j are neighbors, and w i ​ j = 0 w_{ij}=0 otherwise. For each experiment, we distributed the data across agents using a Dirichlet distribution to ensure heterogeneity. In each experiment, we randomly divided the agents into two groups, A and B, containing two and three agents, respectively. Each agent i i in group A participates in Algorithm 1 using manipulated gradients m i , t A = a i , t A ​ g i ​ ( θ i , t ) + b i , t A ​ ξ i , t m_{i,t}^{A}=a_{i,t}^{A}g_{i}(\theta_{i,t})+b_{i,t}^{A}\xi_{i,t} , where each element of ξ i , t \xi_{i,t} is drawn from a Laplace distribution with zero mean and unit variance. Agents in group B act truthfully. It is clear that when a i , t A = 1 a_{i,t}^{A}=1 and b i , t A = 0 b_{i,t}^{A}=0 hold for all i ∈ [ N ] i\in[N] and t ≥ 0 t\geq 0 , all agents act truthfully. For each experiment, we first compared the average net utility of agents in group A under varying scaling factors a A a_{A} for different payment coefficients C C . Then, we conducted a similar comparison under different noise factors b A b_{A} and payment coefficients C C . Finally, we evaluated the training losses and test accuracies in three cases: 1) Algorithm 1 without manipulation (called DSGD (no strategic behaviors)), 2) Algorithm 1 with manipulation under Mechanism 1 (called Algorithm 1 with payment), and 3) Algorithm 1 with manipulation without Mechanism 1 (called Algorithm 1 without payment). In this comparison, each agent in group A strategically selects ( a i , t A , b i , t A ) (a_{i,t}^{A},b_{i,t}^{A}) to empirically maximize its net utility under a preset C t = 10 − 6 ​ κ t 2 δ 2 ​ ( t + 1 ) − 2 ​ v C_{t}=\frac{10^{-6}\kappa_{t}^{2}}{\delta^{2}(t+1)^{-2v}} , where κ t \kappa_{t} and δ \delta are specified for each experiment below.

Image classification using the FeMNIST dataset. We conducted experiments using the FeMNIST dataset, which is a variant of EMNIST and contains 817 , 851 817,851 grayscale handwritten characters of size 28 × 28 28\times 28 across 62 62 classes (digits 0 – 9 9 , uppercase letters A–Z, lowercase letters a–z). In this experiment, we trained a two-layer convolutional neural network following the LEAF architecture [ 6 ] . The model consists of two convolutional layers with 32 32 and 64 64 filters (both using 5 × 5 5\times 5 kernels), each followed by ReLU activation and 2 × 2 2\times 2 max pooling. The extracted features are then flattened and fed into a fully connected layer with 2048 2048 hidden units, followed by a 62 62 -class output layer.
We partitioned the training data among agents according to a Dirichlet distribution with parameter 0.5 0.5 . We used a batch size of 32 32 . We set the learning rate (stepsize) as λ t = 0.1 ​ ( t + 1 ) − 0.55 \lambda_{t}=0.1(t+1)^{-0.55} and
the parameters in payment coefficient C t C_{t} as κ t = ( t + 1 ) − 0.51 \kappa_{t}=(t+1)^{-0.51} and δ = 10 − 4 \delta=10^{-4} . These parameter choices satisfy the conditions of Lemma 1 , Theorem 1 , and Theorem 2 .

Next-character prediction on the Shakespeare dataset. We trained a long short-term memory (LSTM) network [ 46 ] using the Shakespeare dataset, which consists of lines from plays written by William Shakespeare and is formulated as a next-character prediction task over a vocabulary of 80 80 characters. In this experiment, we used 3 , 982 , 028 3,982,028 sequences from 1 , 080 1,080 users for training. Each input character is first mapped to an 8 8 -dimensional embedding vector. The embedded sequence is then processed by a two-layer LSTM, with each layer comprising 256 hidden units and a dropout rate of 0.5 applied between layers. The output from the final LSTM layer at the last time step is passed through a fully connected layer to produce logits over the 80 80 -character vocabulary. This model setup enables the network to capture the sequential dependencies in Shakespearean text for effective character-level prediction.

In Fig. 1 , the top orange lines ( C = 0 C=0 ) show that without our payment mechanism, increasing either a A a_{A} or b A b_{A} raises the average net utility of group-A agents. However, introducing payments ( C > 0 C>0 ) effectively reduces the gains from such strategic behaviors by agents in group A. Moreover, a large C C makes truthful participation the optimal action for group-A agents. Fig. 2 shows that the strategic behavior of agents increases the training loss and decreases the test accuracy in conventional distributed learning algorithms, whereas our payment mechanism mitigates this degradation. This demonstrates the effectiveness of our approach in preserving the learning accuracy of Algorithm 1 despite strategic manipulation.

sections.heading: VIConclusion
sections.text:
We propose the first fully distributed incentive mechanism for distributed stochastic gradient descent with strategic agents, without relying on any centralized server or aggregator. This represents a significant advance since all existing truthfulness approaches require the assistance of a centralized server in computation or execution. Our payment mechanism ensures that the cumulative gain from strategic manipulation remains finite, even over an infinite time horizon—a property unattainable in most existing truthfulness results. Moreover, unlike most existing truthfulness results, our payment mechanism is budget-balanced and guarantees accurate convergence of distributed stochastic gradient descent under strategic manipulation. The results apply to both general convex and strongly convex objective functions, extending beyond existing work that focuses solely on the strongly convex case.
Experimental results on two distributed machine learning applications confirm the effectiveness of our approach.

According to Algorithm 1 , we have θ i , t + 1 ′ = θ i , t + 1 − λ t ​ ( a i , t − 1 ) ​ g i ​ ( θ i , t ) − λ t ​ b i , t ​ ξ i , t \theta_{i,t+1}^{\prime}=\theta_{i,t+1}-\lambda_{t}(a_{i,t}-1)g_{i}(\theta_{i,t})-\lambda_{t}b_{i,t}\xi_{i,t} , which implies N ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ θ ¯ t + 1 − θ ¯ t + 1 ′ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle N\mathbb{E}[\|\bar{\theta}_{t+1}-\bar{\theta}_{t+1}^{\prime}\|^{2}] (51) + ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ θ i , t + 1 − θ i , t + 1 ′ − ( θ ¯ t + 1 − θ ¯ t + 1 ′ ) ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle+\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}\left[\|\theta_{i,t+1}-\theta_{i,t+1}^{\prime}-(\bar{\theta}_{t+1}-\bar{\theta}_{t+1}^{\prime})\|^{2}\right] ≤ 3 ​ λ t 2 ​ ( 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ( a i , t − 1 ) ​ g i ​ ( θ i , t ) ‖ 2 ] + 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ b i , t ​ ξ i , t ‖ 2 ] ) , \displaystyle\leq 3\lambda_{t}^{2}\left(\mathbb{E}\left[\|(a_{i,t}-1)g_{i}(\theta_{i,t})\|^{2}\right]+\mathbb{E}\left[\|b_{i,t}\xi_{i,t}\|^{2}\right]\right), where in the derivation we have used 1 + N − 1 N + ( N − 1 N ) 2 < 3 1+\frac{N-1}{N}+\left(\frac{N-1}{N}\right)^{2}<3 .

By combining ( 27 ) from Lemma 4 and ( 51 ), we obtain 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ θ i , T + 1 − θ i , T + 1 ′ ‖ 2 ] ≤ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 ​ λ t 2 \displaystyle\mathbb{E}[\|\theta_{i,T+1}-\theta_{i,T+1}^{\prime}\|^{2}]\leq 6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}\lambda_{t}^{2} (52) × ( 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ( a i , t − 1 ) ​ g i ​ ( θ i , t ) ‖ 2 ] + 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ b i , t ​ ξ i , t ‖ 2 ] ) . \displaystyle\quad\times\left(\mathbb{E}[\|(a_{i,t}-1)g_{i}(\theta_{i,t})\|^{2}]+\mathbb{E}[\|b_{i,t}\xi_{i,t}\|^{2}]\right). which, combined with Assumption 1 -(i), leads to | 𝔼 ​ [ R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ′ ) ) − R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ) ) ] | ≤ L R , i ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 ​ λ t \displaystyle|\mathbb{E}[R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}^{\prime}))-R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}))]|\leq L_{R,i}\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}\lambda_{t} × ( 𝔼 ​ [ ( a i , t − 1 ) 2 ​ ‖ g i ​ ( θ i , t ) ‖ 2 ] + 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ b i , t ​ ξ i , t ‖ 2 ] ) . \displaystyle\times\left(\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[(a_{i,t}-1)^{2}\|g_{i}(\theta_{i,t})\|^{2}]}+\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[\|b_{i,t}\xi_{i,t}\|^{2}]}\right). By using the preceding inequality and ( 50 ), we obtain 𝔼 ​ [ R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ′ ) ) − P i , t ′ ] − 𝔼 ​ [ R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ) ) − P i , t ] \displaystyle\!\!\!\mathbb{E}\left[R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}^{\prime}))-P_{i,t}^{\prime}\right]-\mathbb{E}\left[R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}))-P_{i,t}\right] (53) ≤ − C t ​ deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ λ t 2 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ( a i , t − 1 ) ​ g i ​ ( θ i , t ) ‖ 2 ] + c 8 ​ deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ C t ​ λ t 2 ​ λ t − 1 2 2 \displaystyle\!\!\!\textstyle\leq\!-\frac{C_{t}\deg(i)\lambda_{t}^{2}}{2}\mathbb{E}[\|(a_{i,t}\!-\!1)g_{i}(\theta_{i,t})\|^{2}]\!+\!\frac{c_{8}\deg(i)C_{t}\lambda_{t}^{2}\lambda_{t-1}^{2}}{2} + L R , i ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 ​ λ t ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ( a i , t − 1 ) ​ g i ​ ( θ i , t ) ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\!\!\!\textstyle\quad+L_{R,i}\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}\lambda_{t}\sqrt{\mathbb{E}\left[\|(a_{i,t}-1)g_{i}(\theta_{i,t})\|^{2}\right]} − C t ​ deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ λ t 2 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ b i , t ​ ξ i , t ‖ 2 ] + c 8 ​ deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ C t ​ λ t 2 ​ λ t − 1 2 2 \displaystyle\!\!\!\textstyle\quad-\frac{C_{t}\deg(i)\lambda_{t}^{2}}{2}\mathbb{E}[\|b_{i,t}\xi_{i,t}\|^{2}]+\frac{c_{8}\deg(i)C_{t}\lambda_{t}^{2}\lambda_{t-1}^{2}}{2} + L R , i ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 ​ λ t ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ b i , t ​ ξ i , t ‖ 2 ] . \displaystyle\!\!\!\textstyle\quad+L_{R,i}\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}\lambda_{t}\sqrt{\mathbb{E}\left[\|b_{i,t}\xi_{i,t}\|^{2}\right]}. It can be seen that the first three terms on the right hand side of ( 53 ) together form a downward-opening quadratic, whose positive root is 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ( a i , t − 1 ) ​ g i ​ ( θ i , t ) ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\!\!\!\textstyle\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[\|(a_{i,t}-1)g_{i}(\theta_{i,t})\|^{2}]} (54) = L R , i ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 + 6 L R , i 2 d t + 1 → T + 1 + c 8 C t 2 deg ( i ) 2 λ t 2 λ t − 1 2 C t ​ deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ λ t \displaystyle\!\!\!\textstyle=\frac{L_{R,i}\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}+\sqrt{6L_{R,i}^{2}d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}+c_{8}C_{t}^{2}\deg(i)^{2}\lambda_{t}^{2}\lambda_{t-1}^{2}}}{C_{t}\deg(i)\lambda_{t}} ≤ 2 ​ L R , i ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 C t ​ deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ λ t + c 8 ​ λ t − 1 . \displaystyle\!\!\!\textstyle\leq\frac{2L_{R,i}\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}}{C_{t}\deg(i)\lambda_{t}}+\sqrt{c_{8}}\lambda_{t-1}. Similarly, the last three terms on the right hand side of ( 53 ) together form a downward-opening quadratic, whose positive root satisfies 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ b i , t ​ ξ i , t ‖ 2 ] ≤ 2 ​ L R , i ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 C t ​ deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ λ t + c 8 ​ λ t − 1 . \displaystyle\!\!\!\textstyle\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[\|b_{i,t}\xi_{i,t}\|^{2}]}\leq\frac{2L_{R,i}\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}}{C_{t}\deg(i)\lambda_{t}}+\sqrt{c_{8}}\lambda_{t-1}. (55)

Further using definition C t = 4 ​ L R , i ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ λ t ​ κ t ​ δ C_{t}=\frac{4L_{R,i}\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}}{\deg(i)\lambda_{t}\kappa_{t}\delta} , we have 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ( a i , t − 1 ) ​ g i ​ ( θ i , t ) ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[\|(a_{i,t}-1)g_{i}(\theta_{i,t})\|^{2}]} ≤ 1 2 ​ κ t ​ δ + c 8 ​ λ t − 1 ; \displaystyle\textstyle\leq\frac{1}{2}\kappa_{t}\delta+\sqrt{c_{8}}\lambda_{t-1}; (56) 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ b i , t ​ ξ i , t ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[\|b_{i,t}\xi_{i,t}\|^{2}]} ≤ 1 2 ​ κ t ​ δ + c 8 ​ λ t − 1 . \displaystyle\textstyle\leq\frac{1}{2}\kappa_{t}\delta+\sqrt{c_{8}}\lambda_{t-1}. Since the decaying rate of λ t \lambda_{t} is higher than that of κ t \kappa_{t} , i.e., r < v r<v , we can choose λ 0 \lambda_{0} such that c 8 ​ λ t − 1 ≤ c 8 ​ λ 0 ≤ 1 2 ​ κ t ​ δ \sqrt{c_{8}}\lambda_{t-1}\leq\sqrt{c_{8}}\lambda_{0}\leq\frac{1}{2}\kappa_{t}\delta holds, which, combined with ( 56 ), proves Lemma 1 .

(i) When f i f_{i} is μ \mu -strongly convex.

By using the dynamics of θ i , t + 1 \theta_{i,t+1} in Algorithm 1 , we obtain 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t + 1 ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] ≤ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ( W ⊗ I n − I N ​ n ) ​ 𝜽 t ′ − λ t ​ 𝒎 t ‖ 2 ] + 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\leq\mathbb{E}[\|(W\otimes I_{n}-I_{Nn})\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\lambda_{t}\boldsymbol{m}_{t}\|^{2}]+\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] + 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ⟨ ( W ⊗ I n − I N ​ n ) ​ 𝜽 t ′ − λ t ​ 𝒎 t , 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ⟩ ] . \displaystyle\textstyle\quad+2\mathbb{E}[\langle(W\otimes I_{n}-I_{Nn})\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}\!-\!\lambda_{t}\boldsymbol{m}_{t},\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\rangle]. (57) Using the relationship W ​ 𝟏 N = 𝟏 N W\boldsymbol{1}_{N}=\boldsymbol{1}_{N} , the first term on the right hand side of ( 57 ) satisfies 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ( W ⊗ I n − I N ​ n ) ​ 𝜽 t ′ − λ t ​ 𝒎 t ‖ 2 ] ≤ 2 ​ λ t 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝒎 t ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\mathbb{E}[\|(W\otimes I_{n}-I_{Nn})\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\lambda_{t}\boldsymbol{m}_{t}\|^{2}]\leq 2\lambda_{t}^{2}\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{m}_{t}\|^{2}] (58) + 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ( W ⊗ I n − I N ​ n ) ​ ( 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ¯ t ′ ) ‖ 2 ] . \displaystyle\quad+\!2\mathbb{E}[\|(W\otimes I_{n}-I_{Nn})(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime})\|^{2}]. By using Assumption 1 and ( 22 ), we have 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝒎 t ‖ 2 ] = ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ a i , t ​ g i ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) − g i ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{m}_{t}\|^{2}]=\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[\|a_{i,t}g_{i}(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})-g_{i}(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})\|^{2}] + ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ g i ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) − ∇ f i ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) ‖ 2 ] + ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ∇ f i ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\quad+\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[\|g_{i}(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})-\nabla f_{i}(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})\|^{2}]\!+\!\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[\|\nabla f_{i}(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})\|^{2}] + ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ b i , t ​ ξ i , t ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\quad+\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[\|b_{i,t}\xi_{i,t}\|^{2}] + ∑ i = 1 N 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ⟨ a i , t ​ g i ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) − g i ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) , ∇ f i ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) ⟩ ] \displaystyle\textstyle\quad+\sum_{i=1}^{N}2\mathbb{E}[\langle a_{i,t}g_{i}(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})-g_{i}(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}),\nabla f_{i}(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})\rangle] ≤ N ​ ( 3 ​ κ t 2 ​ δ 2 + σ 2 ) + 4 ​ H 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\leq N(3\kappa_{t}^{2}\delta^{2}+\sigma^{2})+4H^{2}\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] + 4 ​ H 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ − 𝜽 ∗ ‖ 2 ] , \displaystyle\textstyle\quad+4H^{2}\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}-\boldsymbol{\theta}^{*}\|^{2}], (59) where θ ∗ \theta^{*} represents an optimal solution to problem ( 1 ) and 𝜽 ∗ \boldsymbol{\theta}^{*} is denoted as 𝜽 ∗ = col ⁡ ( θ 1 ∗ , ⋯ , θ N ∗ ) \boldsymbol{\theta}^{*}=\operatorname{col}(\theta_{1}^{*},\cdots,\theta_{N}^{*}) .

Using the relation W ​ 𝟏 N ⊤ = 𝟏 N W\boldsymbol{1}_{N}^{\top}=\boldsymbol{1}_{N} , the third term on the right hand side of ( 57 ) satisfies 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ⟨ ( W ⊗ I n − I N ​ n ) ​ 𝜽 t ′ − λ t ​ 𝒎 t , 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ⟩ ] \displaystyle 2\mathbb{E}[\langle(W\otimes I_{n}-I_{Nn})\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\lambda_{t}\boldsymbol{m}_{t},\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\rangle] = 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ( 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ) ⊤ ​ ( W ⊗ I n − I N ​ n ) ​ ( 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ) ] \displaystyle=2\mathbb{E}\left[(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*})^{\top}(W\otimes I_{n}-I_{Nn})(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*})\right] − 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ⟨ λ t ​ 𝒎 t , 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ⟩ ] \displaystyle\textstyle\quad-2\mathbb{E}[\langle\lambda_{t}\boldsymbol{m}_{t},\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\rangle] ≤ 2 ​ ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ⟨ − λ t ​ ( a i , t ​ g i ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) + b i , t ​ ξ i , t ) , θ i , t ′ − θ ∗ ⟩ ] , \displaystyle\textstyle\leq 2\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}\left[\left\langle-\lambda_{t}(a_{i,t}g_{i}(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})+b_{i,t}\xi_{i,t}),\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}-\theta^{*}\right\rangle\right], (60) where in the derivation we have omitted the negative term 𝔼 ​ [ ( 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ) ⊤ ​ ( W ⊗ I n − I N ​ n ) ​ ( 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ) ] \mathbb{E}\left[(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*})^{\top}(W\otimes I_{n}-I_{Nn})(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*})\right] .

By using the Young’s inequality and the relations 𝔼 ​ [ ξ i , t ] = 0 \mathbb{E}[\xi_{i,t}]=0 and 𝔼 ​ [ g i ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) ] = ∇ f i ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) \mathbb{E}[g_{i}(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})]=\nabla f_{i}(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}) , we have 2 ​ λ t ​ ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ⟨ − ( a i , t ​ g i ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) + b i , t ​ ξ i , t ) + ∇ f i ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) , θ i , t ′ − θ ∗ ⟩ ] \displaystyle\textstyle 2\lambda_{t}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[\langle-(a_{i,t}g_{i}(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})+b_{i,t}\xi_{i,t})+\nabla f_{i}(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}),\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}-\theta^{*}\rangle] − 2 ​ λ t ​ ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ⟨ ∇ f i ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) , θ i , t ′ − θ ∗ ⟩ ] \displaystyle\textstyle\quad-2\lambda_{t}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[\langle\nabla f_{i}(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}),\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}-\theta^{*}\rangle] ≤ 4 ​ N ​ λ t ​ κ t 2 ​ δ 2 μ + μ ​ λ t 4 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\leq\frac{4N\lambda_{t}\kappa_{t}^{2}\delta^{2}}{\mu}+\frac{\mu\lambda_{t}}{4}\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] + 4 ​ H 2 ​ λ t μ ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ¯ t ′ ‖ 2 ] + μ ​ λ t 4 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\quad+\frac{4H^{2}\lambda_{t}}{\mu}\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}\|^{2}]\!+\!\frac{\mu\lambda_{t}}{4}\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] − 2 ​ ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ⟨ λ t ​ ∇ f i ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) , θ i , t ′ − θ ¯ t ′ ⟩ ] \displaystyle\textstyle\quad-2\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[\langle\lambda_{t}\nabla f_{i}(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}),\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}-\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}\rangle] − 2 ​ N ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ⟨ λ t ​ ( ∇ F ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) − ∇ F ​ ( θ ∗ ) ) , θ ¯ t ′ − θ ∗ ⟩ ] . \displaystyle\quad-2N\mathbb{E}[\langle\lambda_{t}(\nabla F(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime})-\nabla F(\theta^{*})),\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\theta^{*}\rangle]. (61)

The fifth term on the right hand side of ( 61 ) satisfies − 2 ​ ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ⟨ λ t ​ ∇ f i ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) , θ i , t ′ − θ ¯ t ′ ⟩ ] \displaystyle\textstyle-2\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[\langle\lambda_{t}\nabla f_{i}(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}),\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}-\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}\rangle] = − 2 ​ ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ⟨ λ t ​ ( ∇ f i ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) − ∇ f i ​ ( θ i ∗ ) ) , θ i , t ′ − θ ¯ t ′ ⟩ ] \displaystyle\textstyle=-2\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[\langle\lambda_{t}\left(\nabla f_{i}(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime})-\nabla f_{i}(\theta_{i}^{*})\right),\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}-\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}\rangle] ≤ 2 ​ λ t 2 ​ H 2 ​ N ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ θ ¯ t ′ − θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] + 2 ​ λ t 2 ​ H 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 ∗ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\leq 2\lambda_{t}^{2}H^{2}N\mathbb{E}[\|\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\theta^{*}\|^{2}]+2\lambda_{t}^{2}H^{2}\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}^{*}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] + 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ¯ t ′ ‖ 2 ] . \displaystyle\textstyle\quad+\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}\|^{2}]. (62)

The μ \mu -strong convexity of f i ​ ( θ ) f_{i}(\theta) implies that the last term on the right hand side of ( 61 ) satisfies − 2 ​ N ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ⟨ λ t ​ ( ∇ F ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) − ∇ F ​ ( θ ∗ ) ) , θ ¯ t ′ − θ ∗ ⟩ ] ≤ − 2 ​ N ​ λ t ​ μ ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ θ ¯ t ′ − θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] -2N\mathbb{E}[\langle\lambda_{t}(\nabla F(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime})-\nabla F(\theta^{*})),\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\theta^{*}\rangle]\leq-2N\lambda_{t}\mu\mathbb{E}[\|\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\theta^{*}\|^{2}] , which, combined with ( 62 ), leads to − 2 ​ ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ⟨ λ t ​ ∇ f i ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) , θ i , t ′ − θ ∗ ⟩ ] \displaystyle\textstyle-2\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[\langle\lambda_{t}\nabla f_{i}(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}),\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}-\theta^{*}\rangle] (63) ≤ − λ t ​ ( μ − 2 ​ H 2 ​ λ t ) ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\leq-\lambda_{t}(\mu-2H^{2}\lambda_{t})\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] + ( 2 ​ λ t ​ ( μ − 2 ​ H 2 ​ λ t ) + 1 ) ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ¯ t ′ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\quad+\left(2\lambda_{t}\left(\mu-2H^{2}\lambda_{t}\right)+1\right)\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}\|^{2}] + 2 ​ H 2 ​ λ t 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ − 𝜽 ∗ ‖ 2 ] , \displaystyle\textstyle\quad+2H^{2}\lambda_{t}^{2}\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}-\boldsymbol{\theta}^{*}\|^{2}], where we have used N ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ θ ¯ t ′ − θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] = ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ θ ¯ t ′ − θ i , t ′ + θ i , t ′ − θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] ≥ 1 2 ​ ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ θ i , t ′ − θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] − ∑ i = 1 N 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ θ i , t ′ − θ ¯ t ′ ‖ 2 ] N\mathbb{E}[\|\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\theta^{*}\|^{2}]=\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[\|\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}+\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}-\theta^{*}\|^{2}]\geq\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[\|\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}-\theta^{*}\|^{2}]-\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[\|\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}-\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}\|^{2}] .

By using the relationship 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 0 ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ¯ 0 ′ ‖ 2 ] = 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 0 − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ¯ 0 ‖ 2 ] \mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\bar{\theta}_{0}^{\prime}\|^{2}]=\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\bar{\theta}_{0}\|^{2}] and iterating ( 79 ) from 0 to t t , we arrive at 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ¯ t ′ ‖ 2 ] ≤ ρ t ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 0 − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ¯ 0 ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}\|^{2}]\leq\rho^{t}\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\bar{\theta}_{0}\|^{2}] (80) + 4 ​ N ​ ( 2 − ρ ) ​ ( 2 ​ δ 2 + σ 2 + L f 2 + 2 ​ δ ​ L f ) ( 1 − ρ ) 2 ​ λ t 2 ≤ c 11 ​ λ t 2 , \displaystyle\textstyle\quad+\frac{4N(2-\rho)(2\delta^{2}+\sigma^{2}+L_{f}^{2}+2\delta L_{f})}{(1-\rho)^{2}}\lambda_{t}^{2}\leq c_{11}\lambda_{t}^{2}, with c 11 = 16 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 0 − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ¯ 0 ‖ 2 ] e 2 ​ ( 1 − ρ ) 2 ​ λ 0 2 + 4 ​ N ​ ( 2 − ρ ) ​ ( 2 ​ δ 2 + σ 2 + L f 2 + 2 ​ δ ​ L f ) ( 1 − ρ ) 2 c_{11}=\frac{16\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\bar{\theta}_{0}\|^{2}]}{e^{2}(1-\rho)^{2}\lambda_{0}^{2}}+\frac{4N(2-\rho)(2\delta^{2}+\sigma^{2}+L_{f}^{2}+2\delta L_{f})}{(1-\rho)^{2}} . Here, in the last inequality, we have used the fact that Lemma 7 in [ 14 ] proves ρ t ≤ 16 e 2 ​ ( ln ⁡ ( ρ ) ) 2 ​ ( t + 1 ) 2 ≤ 16 ​ λ t 2 e 2 ​ ( ln ⁡ ( ρ ) ) 2 ​ λ 0 2 \rho^{t}\leq\frac{16}{e^{2}(\ln(\rho))^{2}(t+1)^{2}}\leq\frac{16\lambda_{t}^{2}}{e^{2}(\ln(\rho))^{2}\lambda_{0}^{2}} , which implies that for any ρ ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \rho\in(0,1) , we have − ln ⁡ ( ρ ) ≥ 1 − ρ -\ln(\rho)\geq 1-\rho .

By substituting ( 78 ) and ( 80 ) into ( 75 ) and using the relationship 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ a i , k ​ g i ​ ( θ i , k ′ ) − g i ​ ( θ i , k ′ ) ‖ 2 ] ≤ κ k 2 ​ δ 2 \mathbb{E}[\|a_{i,k}g_{i}(\theta_{i,k}^{\prime})-g_{i}(\theta_{i,k}^{\prime})\|^{2}]\leq\kappa_{k}^{2}\delta^{2} , we obtain ∑ k = 0 t Φ k \displaystyle\textstyle\sum_{k=0}^{t}\Phi_{k} ≤ ∑ k = 0 t ( N ​ λ 0 2 ​ δ 2 ( k + 1 ) 2 ​ v + 2 ​ r − u + c 12 ( k + 1 ) 2 ​ v ) , \displaystyle\textstyle\leq\sum_{k=0}^{t}\left(\frac{N\lambda_{0}^{2}\delta^{2}}{(k+1)^{2v+2r-u}}+\frac{c_{12}}{(k+1)^{2v}}\right), (81) with c 12 = 4 ​ N ​ δ 2 + 2 ​ N ​ σ 2 + 2 ​ N ​ L f 2 + 4 ​ N ​ δ ​ L f + c 3 ​ ( 2 + λ 0 2 ​ H 2 ) c_{12}=4N\delta^{2}+2N\sigma^{2}+2NL_{f}^{2}+4N\delta L_{f}+c_{3}(2+\lambda_{0}^{2}H^{2}) . Here, we have used the relation λ k 2 β k = λ 0 2 ( k + 1 ) 2 ​ v − u ≤ λ 0 2 \frac{\lambda_{k}^{2}}{\beta_{k}}=\frac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{(k+1)^{2v-u}}\leq\lambda_{0}^{2} .

By using the following inequality: ∑ k = 0 t 1 ( k + 1 ) u ≤ 1 + ∫ k = 1 ∞ 1 x u ​ 𝑑 x ≤ u u − 1 , \textstyle\sum_{k=0}^{t}\frac{1}{(k+1)^{u}}\leq 1+\int_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{x^{u}}dx\leq\frac{u}{u-1}, (82) which is true for any u > 1 u>1 , we can rewrite ( 81 ) as follows: ∑ k = 0 t Φ k ≤ ( 2 ​ v + 2 ​ r − u ) ​ N ​ λ 0 2 ​ δ 2 2 ​ v + 2 ​ r − u − 1 + 2 ​ v ​ c 12 2 ​ v − 1 ≜ c 13 , \textstyle\sum_{k=0}^{t}\Phi_{k}\leq\frac{(2v+2r-u)N\lambda_{0}^{2}\delta^{2}}{2v+2r-u-1}+\frac{2vc_{12}}{2v-1}\triangleq c_{13}, (83) where the constant c 12 c_{12} is given in ( 81 ).

Substituting ( 83 ) into ( 76 ), we can arrive at 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t + 1 ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] (84) ≤ e 2 ​ β 0 ​ ( u − 1 ) + 1 u − 1 ​ ( 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 0 ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] + c 13 ) . \displaystyle\textstyle\leq e^{\frac{2\beta_{0}(u-1)+1}{u-1}}\left(\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}]+c_{13}\right).

We proceed to sum both sides of ( 74 ) from 0 to T T : ∑ t = 0 T 2 ​ N ​ λ t ​ 𝔼 ​ [ F ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) − F ​ ( θ ∗ ) ] \displaystyle\textstyle\sum_{t=0}^{T}2N\lambda_{t}\mathbb{E}[F(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime})-F(\theta^{*})] (85) ≤ − ∑ t = 0 T 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t + 1 ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\leq-\sum_{t=0}^{T}\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] + ∑ t = 0 T ( 1 + 2 ​ β t ) ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] + ∑ t = 0 T Φ t . \displaystyle\textstyle\quad+\sum_{t=0}^{T}(1+2\beta_{t})\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}]+\sum_{t=0}^{T}\Phi_{t}. By using β 0 = 1 \beta_{0}=1 , the first and second terms on the right hand side of ( 85 ) can be simplified as follows: ∑ t = 0 T ( 1 + 2 ​ β t ) ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\sum_{t=0}^{T}(1+2\beta_{t})\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] − ∑ t = 0 T 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t + 1 ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\quad-\sum_{t=0}^{T}\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] ≤ 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 0 ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] + ∑ t = 1 T 2 ​ β t ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\leq 2\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}]+\sum_{t=1}^{T}2\beta_{t}\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] + 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 0 ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] − 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 t + 1 ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\quad+\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}]-\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] ≤ ∑ t = 1 T 2 ( t + 1 ) u ​ ( e 2 ​ ( u − 1 ) + 1 u − 1 ​ ( 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 0 ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] + c 13 ) ) \displaystyle\textstyle\leq\sum_{t=1}^{T}\frac{2}{(t+1)^{u}}\left(e^{\frac{2(u-1)+1}{u-1}}\left(\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}]+c_{13}\right)\right) + 3 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 0 ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\quad+3\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0}^{\prime}-\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}] ≤ ( 2 ​ u ​ e 2 ​ u − 1 u − 1 u − 1 + 3 ) ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ 𝜽 0 ′ − 𝟏 N ⊗ θ ∗ ‖ 2 ] + 2 ​ c 13 ​ u u − 1 ≜ c 14 , \displaystyle\textstyle\leq\left(\frac{2ue^{\frac{2u-1}{u-1}}}{u-1}+3\right)\mathbb{E}[\|\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0}^{\prime}\!-\!\boldsymbol{1}_{N}\otimes\theta^{*}\|^{2}]\!+\!\frac{2c_{13}u}{u-1}\triangleq c_{14}, (86) where we have used ( 84 ) in the second inequality and ( 82 ) in the last inequality.

Substituting ( 83 ) and ( 86 ) into ( 85 ), and using λ T ≤ λ t \lambda_{T}\leq\lambda_{t} for any t ∈ [ 0 , T ] t\in[0,T] , we have ∑ t = 0 T 2 ​ N ​ λ t ​ 𝔼 ​ [ F ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) − F ​ ( θ ∗ ) ] ≤ c 13 + c 14 , \sum_{t=0}^{T}2N\lambda_{t}\mathbb{E}[F(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime})-F(\theta^{*})]\leq c_{13}+c_{14}, which further implies 1 T + 1 ​ ∑ t = 0 T 𝔼 ​ [ F ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) − F ​ ( θ ∗ ) ] ≤ C 2 N ​ ( T + 1 ) 1 − v , \textstyle\frac{1}{T+1}\sum_{t=0}^{T}\mathbb{E}\left[F(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime})-F(\theta^{*})\right]\leq\frac{C_{2}}{N(T+1)^{1-v}}, (87) where C 2 = c 13 + c 14 2 ​ λ 0 C_{2}\!=\!\frac{c_{13}+c_{14}}{2\lambda_{0}} with c 13 c_{13} given in ( 83 ) and c 6 c_{6} given in ( 86 ).

Assumption 1 implies 𝔼 ​ [ F ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) − F ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) ] ≤ L f ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ θ i , t ′ − θ ¯ t ′ ‖ ] \mathbb{E}[F(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})-F(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime})]\leq L_{f}\mathbb{E}[\|\theta_{i,t}^{\prime}-\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime}\|] . By using ( 80 ), we have 𝔼 ​ [ F ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) − F ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) ] ≤ L f ​ c 11 ​ λ 0 ( t + 1 ) v . \textstyle\mathbb{E}\left[F(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})-F(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime})\right]\leq\frac{L_{f}\sqrt{c_{11}}\lambda_{0}}{(t+1)^{v}}. (88) Since ∑ t = 0 T 1 ( t + 1 ) p ≤ ∫ x = 0 T + 1 1 x p ​ 𝑑 x ≤ ( T + 1 ) 1 − p 1 − p \sum_{t=0}^{T}\frac{1}{(t+1)^{p}}\leq\int_{x=0}^{T+1}\frac{1}{x^{p}}dx\leq\frac{(T+1)^{1-p}}{1-p} always holds for any p ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) p\in(0,1) , we arrive at 1 T + 1 ​ ∑ t = 0 T 𝔼 ​ [ F ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) − F ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) ] ≤ L f ​ c 11 ​ λ 0 ( 1 − v ) ​ ( T + 1 ) v . \textstyle\frac{1}{T+1}\sum_{t=0}^{T}\mathbb{E}\left[F(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})-F(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime})\right]\leq\frac{L_{f}\sqrt{c_{11}}\lambda_{0}}{(1-v)(T+1)^{v}}. (89)

By combining ( 87 ) and ( 89 ), we arrive at 1 T + 1 ​ ∑ t = 0 T 𝔼 ​ [ F ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) − F ​ ( θ ∗ ) ] ≤ C 3 ​ ( T + 1 ) − ( 1 − v ) , \textstyle\frac{1}{T+1}\sum_{t=0}^{T}\mathbb{E}\left[F(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})-F(\theta^{*})\right]\leq C_{3}(T+1)^{-(1-v)}, (90) where the constant C 3 C_{3} is given by C 3 = N ​ L f ​ c 11 ​ λ 0 1 − v + C 2 C_{3}=\frac{NL_{f}\sqrt{c_{11}}\lambda_{0}}{1-v}+C_{2} with c 11 c_{11} given in ( 80 ) and C 2 C_{2} given in ( 87 ).

The definition of C 3 C_{3} implies C 3 ≤ 𝒪 ​ ( H 2 ​ ( σ 2 + L f 2 + δ 2 ) ( 1 − ρ ) 2 ) C_{3}\leq\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{H^{2}(\sigma^{2}+L_{f}^{2}+\delta^{2})}{(1-\rho)^{2}}\right) , which, combined with ( 90 ), proves Theorem 1 -(ii).

To prove Theorem 2 , we introduce the following auxiliary lemma, which is used in the analysis of strongly convex objective functions, while it is not needed in the convex case.

We are now in a position to prove Theorem 2 .

Proof of Theorem 2.

(i) When f i f_{i} is μ \mu -strongly convex.

By using Assumption 1 -(i) and ( 91 ), we obtain ( 𝔼 ​ [ | R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ′ ) ) − R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ) ) | ] ) 2 \displaystyle\textstyle(\mathbb{E}[|R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}^{\prime}))-R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}))|])^{2} ≤ L R , i 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ θ i , T + 1 ′ − θ i , T + 1 ‖ 2 ] ≤ L R , i 2 ​ C 4 ​ λ T . \displaystyle\textstyle\leq L_{R,i}^{2}\mathbb{E}[\|\theta_{i,T+1}^{\prime}-\theta_{i,T+1}\|^{2}]\leq L_{R,i}^{2}C_{4}\lambda_{T}.

By using Lyapunov’s inequality for moments, we have 𝔼 ​ [ | R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ′ ) ) − R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ) ) | ] ≤ L R , i ​ D 1 ​ λ T . \textstyle\mathbb{E}[|R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}^{\prime}))-R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}))|]\leq L_{R,i}\sqrt{D_{1}\lambda_{T}}. (105) According to ( 50 ), we have 𝔼 ​ [ P i , t − P i , t ′ ] ≤ − C t ​ deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ λ t 2 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ( a i , t − 1 ) ​ g i ​ ( θ i , t ) ‖ 2 ] \displaystyle\textstyle\mathbb{E}\left[P_{i,t}-P_{i,t}^{\prime}\right]\leq-\frac{C_{t}\deg(i)\lambda_{t}^{2}}{2}\mathbb{E}[\|(a_{i,t}-1)g_{i}(\theta_{i,t})\|^{2}] (106) − C t ​ deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ λ t 2 2 ​ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ b i , t ​ ξ i , t ‖ 2 ] + c 8 ​ deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ C t ​ λ t 2 ​ λ t − 1 2 . \displaystyle\textstyle\quad-\frac{C_{t}\deg(i)\lambda_{t}^{2}}{2}\mathbb{E}[\|b_{i,t}\xi_{i,t}\|^{2}]+c_{8}\deg(i)C_{t}\lambda_{t}^{2}\lambda_{t-1}^{2}. Summing both sides of ( 106 ) from t = 1 t=1 to t = T t=T and using ( 22 ) yield ∑ t = 1 T 𝔼 ​ [ P i , t − P i , t ′ ] ≤ − deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ ∑ t = 1 T C t ​ λ t 2 ​ κ t 2 ​ δ 2 \displaystyle\textstyle\sum_{t=1}^{T}\mathbb{E}[P_{i,t}-P_{i,t}^{\prime}]\leq-\deg(i)\sum_{t=1}^{T}C_{t}\lambda_{t}^{2}\kappa_{t}^{2}\delta^{2} (107) − deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ ∑ t = 1 T c 8 ​ C t ​ λ t 2 ​ λ t − 1 2 ≤ − 2 ​ deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ ∑ t = 1 T C t ​ λ t 2 ​ κ t 2 ​ δ 2 , \displaystyle\textstyle-\deg(i)\sum_{t=1}^{T}c_{8}C_{t}\lambda_{t}^{2}\lambda_{t-1}^{2}\leq-2\deg(i)\sum_{t=1}^{T}C_{t}\lambda_{t}^{2}\kappa_{t}^{2}\delta^{2}, where in the derivation we have used the fact that the decaying rate of λ t 2 ​ λ t − 1 2 \lambda_{t}^{2}\lambda_{t-1}^{2} is faster than λ t 2 ​ κ t 2 \lambda_{t}^{2}\kappa_{t}^{2} and c 8 ≥ 1 c_{8}\geq 1 .

Combining ( 105 ) and ( 107 ), and using the definition C t = 4 ​ L R , i ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ λ t ​ κ t ​ δ C_{t}=\frac{4L_{R,i}\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}}{\deg(i)\lambda_{t}\kappa_{t}\delta} , we have 𝔼 ​ [ R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ′ ) ) − ∑ t = 1 T P i , t ′ ] − 𝔼 ​ [ R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ) ) − ∑ t = 1 T P i , t ] \displaystyle\textstyle\mathbb{E}[R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}^{\prime}))\!-\!\sum_{t=1}^{T}P_{i,t}^{\prime}]\!-\!\mathbb{E}[R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}))\!-\!\sum_{t=1}^{T}P_{i,t}] ≤ L R , i ​ D 1 ​ λ 0 ( T + 1 ) v 2 + 2 ​ deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ ∑ t = 1 T 4 ​ L R , i ​ λ 0 ​ δ ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ ( t + 1 ) v + r \displaystyle\textstyle\leq\frac{L_{R,i}\sqrt{D_{1}\lambda_{0}}}{(T+1)^{\frac{v}{2}}}+2\deg(i)\sum_{t=1}^{T}\frac{4L_{R,i}\lambda_{0}\delta\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}}{\deg(i)(t+1)^{v+r}} ≤ L R , i ​ D 1 ​ λ 0 ( T + 1 ) v 2 + 8 ​ ( v + r ) ​ L R , i ​ λ 0 ​ δ ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 v + r − 1 , \displaystyle\textstyle\quad\leq\frac{L_{R,i}\sqrt{D_{1}\lambda_{0}}}{(T+1)^{\frac{v}{2}}}+\frac{8(v+r)L_{R,i}\lambda_{0}\delta\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}}{v+r-1}, (108) where we have used ( 82 ) in the last inequality. Eq. ( 108 ) implies Theorem 2 .

(ii) When f i ​ ( θ ) f_{i}(\theta) is general convex.

From Theorem 1 -(ii), we have 1 T + 1 ​ ∑ t = 0 T 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ∇ F ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) ‖ 2 ] ≤ 𝒪 ​ ( 1 ( T + 1 ) 1 − v ) \frac{1}{T+1}\sum_{t=0}^{T}\mathbb{E}[\|\nabla F(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime})\|^{2}]\leq\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{(T+1)^{1-v}}\right) . According to the Stolz–Cesàro theorem, we obtain lim t → ∞ 1 t ​ ∑ k = 0 t − 1 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ∇ F ​ ( θ ¯ k ′ ) ‖ 2 ] = 0 \displaystyle\textstyle\textstyle\lim_{t\rightarrow\infty}\frac{1}{t}\sum_{k=0}^{t-1}\mathbb{E}\left[\|\nabla F(\bar{\theta}_{k}^{\prime})\|^{2}\right]=0 (109) ⟹ lim t → ∞ 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ∇ F ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) ‖ 2 ] = 0 . \displaystyle\textstyle\Longrightarrow\lim_{t\rightarrow\infty}\mathbb{E}[\|\nabla F(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime})\|^{2}]=0.

We denote Θ ∗ = { θ ∈ ℝ n ∣ ∇ F ​ ( θ ) = 0 } \Theta^{*}=\{\theta\in\mathbb{R}^{n}\mid\nabla F(\theta)=0\} as the optimal-solution set. By using the continuity of ∇ F ​ ( θ ) \nabla F(\theta) and ( 109 ), we have lim t → ∞ d ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ , Θ ∗ ) = 0 \lim_{t\rightarrow\infty}\mathrm{d}(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime},\Theta^{*})=0 , where d ​ ( θ , Θ ∗ ) = inf y ∈ Θ ∗ ‖ θ − y ‖ \mathrm{d}(\theta,\Theta^{*})=\inf_{y\in\Theta^{*}}\|\theta-y\| denotes the distance from θ \theta to Θ ∗ \Theta^{*} . Furthermore, by using the continuity of f i ​ ( θ ) f_{i}(\theta) , we have lim t → ∞ f i ​ ( θ ¯ t ′ ) = f i ​ ( θ ′ ⁣ ∗ ) \lim_{t\rightarrow\infty}f_{i}(\bar{\theta}_{t}^{\prime})=f_{i}(\theta^{\prime*}) for some θ ′ ⁣ ∗ ∈ Θ ∗ \theta^{\prime*}\in\Theta^{*} .

Given that m i , t = ∇ f i ​ ( θ i , t ) m_{i,t}=\nabla f_{i}(\theta_{i,t}) is a special case of m i , t = a i , t ​ ∇ f i ​ ( θ i , t ′ ) + b i , t ​ ξ i , t m_{i,t}=a_{i,t}\nabla f_{i}(\theta_{i,t}^{\prime})+b_{i,t}\xi_{i,t} with a i , t ≥ 1 a_{i,t}\geq 1 and b i , t ∈ ℝ b_{i,t}\in\mathbb{R} , we have lim t → ∞ f i ​ ( θ ¯ t ) = f i ​ ( θ ∗ ) \lim_{t\rightarrow\infty}f_{i}(\bar{\theta}_{t})=f_{i}(\theta^{*}) for some θ ∗ ∈ Θ ∗ \theta^{*}\in\Theta^{*} . Note that when F ​ ( θ ) F(\theta) is convex, the optimal solutions θ ∗ \theta^{*} and θ ′ ⁣ ∗ \theta^{\prime*} may be different elements in Θ ∗ \Theta^{*} .

We proceed to prove that for any two points θ 1 ∗ , θ 2 ∗ ∈ X ∗ \theta_{1}^{*},\theta_{2}^{*}\in X^{*} , the relationship f i ​ ( θ 1 ∗ ) = f i ​ ( θ 2 ∗ ) f_{i}(\theta_{1}^{*})=f_{i}(\theta_{2}^{*}) always holds. Since F ​ ( θ ) F(\theta) is convex, its optimal-solution set Θ ∗ \Theta^{*} is convex, closed, and connected. We choose some point θ 0 ∗ ∈ Θ ∗ \theta_{0}^{*}\in\Theta^{*} and consider a direction b ∈ ℝ n b\in\mathbb{R}^{n} such that the segment θ 0 ∗ + ς ​ b \theta_{0}^{*}+\varsigma b lies within Θ ∗ \Theta^{*} for an arbitrarily small ς > 0 \varsigma>0 . Since F ​ ( θ ) F(\theta) is constant over Θ ∗ \Theta^{*} , its first and second directional derivatives at θ 0 ∗ \theta_{0}^{*} are zero, i.e., ⟨ b , ∇ F ​ ( θ 0 ∗ ) ⟩ = 0 \langle b,\nabla F(\theta_{0}^{*})\rangle=0 and b ⊤ ​ ∇ 2 F ​ ( θ 0 ∗ ) ​ b = 0 b^{\top}\nabla^{2}F(\theta_{0}^{*})b=0 . Recalling F ​ ( θ ) = 1 N ​ ∑ i = 1 N f i ​ ( θ ) F(\theta)=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}f_{i}(\theta) , we have b ⊤ ​ 1 N ​ ∑ i = 1 N ∇ 2 f i ​ ( θ 0 ∗ ) ​ b = 0 b^{\top}\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\nabla^{2}f_{i}(\theta_{0}^{*})b=0 . Furthermore, since each f i f_{i} is convex and twice differentiable, we have ∇ 2 f i ​ ( θ 0 ∗ ) ⪰ 0 \nabla^{2}f_{i}(\theta_{0}^{*})\succeq 0 and b ⊤ ​ ∇ 2 f i ​ ( θ 0 ∗ ) ​ b ⪰ 0 b^{\top}\nabla^{2}f_{i}(\theta_{0}^{*})b\succeq 0 , which, combined with b ⊤ ​ ∇ 2 F ​ ( θ 0 ∗ ) ​ b = 0 b^{\top}\nabla^{2}F(\theta_{0}^{*})b=0 , leads to b ⊤ ​ ∇ 2 f i ​ ( θ 0 ∗ ) ​ b = 0 b^{\top}\nabla^{2}f_{i}(\theta_{0}^{*})b=0 . Hence, each f i ​ ( θ ) f_{i}(\theta) has zero second directional derivative along any direction in Θ ∗ \Theta^{*} , meaning that f i ​ ( θ ) f_{i}(\theta) is constant on Θ ∗ \Theta^{*} . Hence, for any θ 1 ∗ , θ 2 ∗ ∈ Θ ∗ \theta_{1}^{*},\theta_{2}^{*}\in\Theta^{*} , we have f i ​ ( θ 1 ∗ ) = f i ​ ( θ 2 ∗ ) f_{i}(\theta_{1}^{*})=f_{i}(\theta_{2}^{*}) , which naturally leads to f i ​ ( θ ′ ⁣ ∗ ) = f i ​ ( θ ∗ ) f_{i}(\theta^{\prime*})=f_{i}(\theta^{*}) .

By using the relation f i ​ ( θ ′ ⁣ ∗ ) = f i ​ ( θ ∗ ) f_{i}(\theta^{\prime*})=f_{i}(\theta^{*}) and ( 90 ), we have lim T → ∞ 𝔼 ​ [ R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ′ ) ) − R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ) ) ] \displaystyle\textstyle\lim_{T\rightarrow\infty}\mathbb{E}[R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}^{\prime}))-R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}))] (110) = 𝔼 ​ [ R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ ′ ⁣ ∗ ) ) − R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ ∗ ) ) ] = 0 . \displaystyle\textstyle=\mathbb{E}[R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta^{\prime*}))-R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta^{*}))]=0.

Based on the definition C t = 4 ​ L R , i ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 deg ⁡ ( i ) ​ λ t ​ κ t ​ δ C_{t}=\frac{4L_{R,i}\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}}{\deg(i)\lambda_{t}\kappa_{t}\delta} and ( 107 ), we obtain ∑ t = 1 T 𝔼 ​ [ P i , t − P i , t ′ ] \displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{T}\mathbb{E}[P_{i,t}-P_{i,t}^{\prime}] ≤ − 2 ​ ∑ t = 1 T 4 ​ L R , i ​ λ 0 ​ δ ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 ( t + 1 ) v + r \displaystyle\textstyle\leq-2\sum_{t=1}^{T}\frac{4L_{R,i}\lambda_{0}\delta\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}}{(t+1)^{v+r}} (111) ≤ 8 ​ ( v + r ) ​ L R , i ​ λ 0 ​ δ ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 v + r − 1 . \displaystyle\textstyle\leq\frac{8(v+r)L_{R,i}\lambda_{0}\delta\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}}{v+r-1}.

Combining ( 110 ) and ( 111 ), we arrive at lim T → ∞ 𝔼 ​ [ R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ′ ) ) − P i , t ′ ] \displaystyle\textstyle\lim_{T\rightarrow\infty}\mathbb{E}[R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}^{\prime}))-P_{i,t}^{\prime}] (112) − lim T → ∞ ∑ t = 1 T 𝔼 ​ [ R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ) ) − ∑ t = 1 T P i , t ] \displaystyle\textstyle\quad-\lim_{T\rightarrow\infty}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\mathbb{E}[R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}))-\sum_{t=1}^{T}P_{i,t}] ≤ 8 ​ ( v + r ) ​ L R , i ​ λ 0 ​ δ ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 v + r − 1 . \displaystyle\textstyle\leq\frac{8(v+r)L_{R,i}\lambda_{0}\delta\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}}{v+r-1}. Furthermore, according to ( 112 ), for any finite T T , there always exists a C > 0 C>0 such that the following inequality holds: 𝔼 ​ [ R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ′ ) ) − ∑ t = 1 T P i , t ′ ] − 𝔼 ​ [ R i ​ ( f i ​ ( θ i , T + 1 ) ) − ∑ t = 1 T P i , t ] \displaystyle\textstyle\mathbb{E}[R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}^{\prime}))\!-\!\sum_{t=1}^{T}P_{i,t}^{\prime}]-\mathbb{E}[R_{i}(f_{i}(\theta_{i,T+1}))\!-\!\sum_{t=1}^{T}P_{i,t}] ≤ C ​ 8 ​ ( v + r ) ​ L R , i ​ λ 0 ​ δ ​ 6 ​ d t + 1 → T + 1 v + r − 1 , \displaystyle\textstyle\leq C\frac{8(v+r)L_{R,i}\lambda_{0}\delta\sqrt{6d_{t+1\text{\tiny$\to$}T+1}}}{v+r-1}, (113) which, combined with ( 112 ), proves Theorem 2 .

sections.heading: VI-EAdditional results
sections.text:
We present the following Lemma 6 to clarify the connection between ε \varepsilon -incentive compatibility and ε \varepsilon -Nash equilibrium. To this end, we first introduce the notion of ε \varepsilon -Nash equilibrium in our game-theoretic framework.

Definition 3 implies that, in an ε \varepsilon -Nash equilibrium, no agent can improve its net utility by more than ε \varepsilon through unilateral deviation from its equilibrium action trajectory.

We present Corollary 1 to prove that any manipulation of model parameters shared among agents in Algorithm 1 corresponds to some form of alteration in the gradient estimates.

We present Corollary 2 to prove that our payment mechanism can ensure lim t → ∞ [ P i , t ] = 0 \lim_{t\rightarrow\infty}[P_{i,t}]=0 . This guarantees that no payment is required from agent i i when it behaves truthfully in an infinite time horizon.
